metadata.subject: Science
metadata.description: Life Sciences 11 is a senior biology course focused on the molecular and cellular basis of life, evolutionary theory at the population level, and the classification and diversity of living organisms. Students conduct inquiry-based investigations and engage with ethical questions in genetics, biotechnology, and taxonomy. First Peoples knowledge of living systems and biodiversity is integrated throughout as a legitimate scientific perspective.
metadata.general_description: A senior biology course covering molecular and cellular biology, evolution and population genetics, and the classification of living organisms — developing inquiry skills and scientific reasoning about life's diversity and continuity.

big_ideas.statement: Life is a result of interactions at the molecular and cellular levels.
big_ideas.elaboration: The functions that define life — energy transformation, reproduction, response, and regulation — arise from the intricate molecular and cellular interactions within and between cells, from DNA replication to enzyme catalysis.

big_ideas.statement: Evolution occurs at the population level.
big_ideas.elaboration: Natural selection and other evolutionary mechanisms act on variation within populations, not individuals, gradually shifting allele frequencies over generations and potentially resulting in new species.

big_ideas.statement: Organisms are grouped based on common characteristics.
big_ideas.elaboration: Classification systems — from the three-domain system to phylogenetic trees — organize the diversity of life based on shared evolutionary history, structural, molecular, and biochemical evidence.

competencies.raw: Demonstrate sustained intellectual curiosity; make observations to identify questions about nature; formulate multiple hypotheses and predict outcomes.
i_can_statements: I can identify complex biological questions about cellular processes, evolution, and organism diversity.
I can make careful biological observations and generate testable hypotheses.
I can predict multiple possible outcomes for a biological investigation and explain my reasoning.

competencies.raw: Collaboratively plan investigations; assess risks and ethical concerns; use appropriate SI units and equipment; apply accuracy and precision concepts including significant figures and scientific notation.
i_can_statements: I can design and plan a biological investigation with appropriate controls and variables.
I can identify and manage safety and ethical risks in biological investigations, including with live organisms.
I can collect accurate data using microscopy, staining, and other biological techniques in SI units.

categories.name: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information
categories.raw: Processing and Analyzing Data and Information

competencies.raw: Interpret local environments; apply First Peoples perspectives; identify patterns and trends; construct graphs and models; draw evidence-based conclusions; analyze cause-and-effect relationships.
i_can_statements: I can apply First Peoples ecological knowledge and perspectives to enrich my understanding of biodiversity.
I can identify patterns and trends in biological data, including population genetics and phylogenetic data.
I can construct and interpret phylogenetic trees, cladograms, and population graphs.
I can draw conclusions about evolutionary and ecological relationships from multiple lines of evidence.

competencies.raw: Assess methods for errors and uncertainties; describe improvements; evaluate model validity; question assumptions and bias; consider evolving knowledge; critically analyze information sources.
i_can_statements: I can identify sources of error in biological investigations and suggest specific improvements.
I can evaluate the limitations of biological models (e.g., the Central Dogma, Hardy-Weinberg).
I can question assumptions and bias in scientific claims about genetics and biotechnology.
I can consider the ethical, social, and environmental implications of genetic modification and biotechnology.

competencies.raw: Contribute to community care; design collaborative projects with local and global connections; solve real-world problems; consider scientists' roles in innovation.
i_can_statements: I can apply biological concepts to address real-world challenges in health, agriculture, or conservation.
I can consider the role of biologists and biotechnologists in solving local and global problems.
I can connect my learning to community and ecosystem stewardship.

competencies.raw: Formulate theoretical models; construct evidence-based arguments; communicate scientific ideas using appropriate language and conventions.
i_can_statements: I can formulate and explain biological models of cellular processes and evolutionary mechanisms.
I can communicate biological ideas using precise scientific vocabulary and correct conventions.
I can write lab reports and papers that present evidence-based biological arguments.

areas.name: Cell Biology and Molecular Life Processes
areas.raw: Life is a result of interactions at the molecular and cellular levels.
topics: Levels of biological organization: molecular, cellular, tissue, organ, organ system, organism
Cell structure and function: prokaryotic vs. eukaryotic; organelles and their roles
Unicellular vs. multicellular organisms; cell specialization and differentiation
DNA structure and the Central Dogma: replication, transcription, and translation
Energy transformations: cellular respiration (aerobic and anaerobic) and photosynthesis
Reproduction at the cellular level: mitosis, meiosis, binary fission, budding, conjugation
Viruses: structure, lytic and lysogenic cycles, and immune response
Cell communication: hormones, receptors, and signal transduction (overview)

areas.name: Evolution
areas.raw: Evolution occurs at the population level.
topics: Mechanisms of evolution: natural selection, genetic drift, gene flow, mutation
Microevolution: changes in allele frequencies within a population over time
Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium: conditions and application as a null model
Adaptation: structural, behavioural, and physiological adaptations
Macroevolution: speciation — allopatric, sympatric; divergent and convergent evolution
Co-evolution: predator-prey, parasite-host, and mutualistic relationships
Evidence for evolution: fossil record, comparative anatomy, molecular biology, biogeography, embryology
Genetic modifications and ethics: gene therapy, GMOs, and CRISPR

areas.name: Biodiversity and Classification
areas.raw: Organisms are grouped based on common characteristics.
topics: Binomial nomenclature and taxonomic hierarchy: domain, kingdom, phylum, class, order, family, genus, species
Three domains of life: Bacteria, Archaea, Eukarya
Kingdoms: Bacteria, Protista, Fungi, Plantae, Animalia — characteristics and examples
Phylogenetic trees and cladograms: constructing and interpreting evolutionary relationships
Dichotomous keys: using and creating keys to identify organisms
Evidence for classification: DNA analysis, biochemistry, anatomy, embryology, fossils
Trends in organism complexity: symmetry, coelom, vascularization, reproductive strategies, seed production
First Peoples classification systems and traditional ecological knowledge of biodiversity
